Article Title: Offline maps, LoRa devices and tear gas hacks: Jantar Mantar protest survival tips trend online
Article Text: NEW DELHI: As protests led by the Cockroach Janata Party continued at Jantar Mantar amid repeated clashes with police, social media has been flooded with "survival guides" offering tips on coping with internet shutdowns, tear gas exposure and police action.From downloading offline maps and using Bluetooth-based messaging apps to carrying antacid solutions for tear gas exposure, demonstrators are increasingly relying on crowdsourced advice to navigate the ongoing agitation.One social media user, who said he had been stranded during repeated internet shutdowns at the protest site, advised participants to download offline maps before arriving. He also recommended installing Bluetooth-based messaging applications that function without mobile data or WiFi, enabling communication even during connectivity disruptions.The user further suggested installing locally run large language models (LLMs) that operate directly on devices instead of cloud-based AI services, allowing access to AI tools even when internet services are suspended.He also advised protesters to carry LoRa-based communication devices, which can enable long-range communication when mobile networks are unavailable, and urged them to save the contact details of at least five people living near the protest venue to use in emergencies.Advice on dealing with tear gas has also gained traction online. A social media user recommended carrying a mixture of equal parts water and an antacid such as Maalox or Digene, claiming it could help reduce the burning sensation and eye irritation caused by tear gas exposure. She said she shared the remedy after a friend found it useful during an earlier protest.Another protester said he carried a painter's mask or an ordinary dust mask along with protective goggles to reduce exposure to smoke and chemical irritants.Some demonstrators have also devised makeshift protective gear against baton charges. One protester said he placed thermocol sheets inside his jeans around the back and hip area to provide cushioning. He added that he carried a spare shirt for himself and a kurta for any female protester in case someone's clothes were torn during the demonstration.The protester also said he carried two helmets, one for himself and another to lend to anyone who needed protection.The trend reflects how demonstrators are increasingly turning to social media for practical advice and real-time information during the protests.On Monday, several students and other protesters were injured in clashes with police during their march to Parliament. One woman was rushed to Ram Manohar Lohia Hospital in critical condition. Several police personnel were also injured in the confrontations.The students, protesting under the banner of the Cockroach Janata Party, have been demanding the resignation of Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the NEET paper leak controversy.
Article Summary: Protesters are increasingly relying on crowdsourced advice to navigate the ongoing agitation. One social media user, who said he had been stranded during repeated internet shutdowns at the protest site, advised participants to download offline maps. He also recommended installing Bluetooth-based messaging applications that function without mobile data or WiFi.

Article Title: Scientists discovered an underwater city of 60-million fish nests beneath Antarctica's ice shelf
Article Text:
Image: AI Generated

A robot explored beneath Antarctica's ice shelf and confirmed a hidden underwater metropolis

Why these transparent icefish build millions of nests beneath the Antarctic ice

A slightly warmer pocket of ocean makes this extraordinary nursery possible

Why Weddell seals turn this breeding ground into a seasonal hunting ground

Why scientists believe the colony deserves stronger protection

Hidden beneath hundreds of metres of Antarctic ice lies one of the planet's most extraordinary wildlife spectacles. After drilling through the Filchner Ice Shelf, scientists deployed an autonomous underwater robot into the dark waters below, where it revealed what can only be described as an underwater city, a sprawling breeding colony covering an area roughly the size of Orlando, Florida. Spread across the seafloor were an estimated 60 million nests, each carefully built and guarded by transparent, antifreeze-blooded Antarctic icefish. Yet this remarkable nursery is also the setting for a relentless struggle for survival, as hungry Weddell seals transform the breeding season into a deadly feeding ground. The discovery is reshaping scientists' understanding of Antarctica's hidden ecosystems and their importance to the Southern Ocean. It also highlights how much of Antarctica's underwater world remains unexplored, with future expeditions likely to uncover even more hidden ecosystems beneath the continent's vast ice shelves.The vast breeding colony lies beneath the Filchner Ice Shelf in the southern Weddell Sea, one of the most remote and inaccessible marine environments on Earth.The colony was first identified during a 2021 expedition led by Dr Autun Purser of the Alfred Wegener Institute (AWI), Helmholtz Centre for Polar and Marine Research , using the Ocean Floor Observation and Bathymetry System (OFOBS).The team's findings revealed an astonishing concentration of nests extending across approximately 240 square kilometres, an area comparable to the size of Orlando. The researchers estimated that the site contains around 60 million active nests, making it the largest fish breeding colony ever discovered.More recently, AWI scientists returned with LASSIE (Low-Altitude Survey System for Icefish), an autonomous underwater vehicle specifically designed to monitor the colony beneath the ice shelf. Operating continuously for 48 hours, LASSIE confirmed that both the colony's size and nesting density have remained remarkably stable since the original discovery, providing researchers with the first long-term baseline for monitoring this unique ecosystem.The colony belongs to the blackfin icefish (Neopagetopsis ionah), one of Antarctica's most unusual fish species. Icefish are famous for lacking haemoglobin, the red protein that carries oxygen in the blood of almost every other vertebrate. As a result, their blood appears almost transparent. They also produce specialised antifreeze glycoproteins that prevent ice crystals from forming inside their bodies, allowing them to survive in seawater that remains below the normal freezing point of fresh water. These remarkable adaptations have enabled the species to thrive in one of the planet's coldest and most extreme marine environments.Each nest is a circular depression measuring roughly 75 centimetres in diameter, cleared of fine sediment to expose small stones that help protect the eggs. Researchers found that a typical nest contains around 1,700 eggs, while an adult fish, usually the male, stands guard until they hatch. Across the colony, nests occur at an average density of about 0.26 nests per square metre, creating an immense network of breeding sites stretching as far as the cameras could see. The study was led by Dr Autun Purser and an international team of researchers from the Alfred Wegener Institute, the University of Bremen, the Max Planck Institute for Marine Microbiology, the University of Basel and other institutions.aScientists discovered that the breeding colony exists in a remarkably specific part of the seafloor, revealing an unexpected hotspot for Antarctic marine reproduction.Measurements showed that modified Warm Deep Water flowing through the Filchner Trough keeps the seabed beneath the colony up to 2°C warmer than surrounding bottom waters. Although still close to freezing, this subtle temperature difference appears sufficient to create ideal breeding conditions for the icefish.The researchers believe this narrow band of relatively warmer water explains why millions of fish concentrate their nests in one location rather than spreading across the Antarctic seafloor. Without these favourable oceanographic conditions, such an enormous breeding aggregation would probably not exist. The finding also highlights how small environmental differences can shape the distribution and reproductive success of entire marine populations over vast areas.The underwater city does not remain peaceful for long.Using satellite tracking data collected over many years, researchers found that Weddell seals (Leptonychotes weddellii) repeatedly travel to the breeding colony during the nesting season. The dense concentration of adult fish provides an abundant food source, making the area one of the seals' most important feeding grounds.Scientists also documented numerous fish carcasses scattered throughout the colony, demonstrating that while millions of icefish gather to reproduce, many become prey before the breeding season ends. This intense predation helps transfer enormous amounts of energy through the Antarctic food web, supporting predators while recycling nutrients back into the surrounding ecosystem.Rather than representing ecological failure, the seasonal "massacre" is a natural process that links one of Earth's largest fish nurseries with the broader Southern Ocean ecosystem.The discovery has transformed scientific understanding of Antarctic marine ecosystems.According to Dr Autun Purser and colleagues, the breeding colony represents not only the largest known fish nesting site on Earth but also one of Antarctica's most significant concentrations of marine biomass. Because millions of breeding fish gather in a single location each year, researchers argue that the area should receive enhanced conservation protection.The findings have already strengthened proposals for expanding Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) in the Weddell Sea. Long-term monitoring with autonomous systems such as LASSIE will also allow scientists to measure how climate change, ocean warming and shifting sea-ice conditions affect one of the planet's most extraordinary hidden ecosystems.Far beneath Antarctica's frozen surface, where sunlight never reaches and temperatures remain near freezing, an underwater city continues to thrive, its millions of carefully guarded nests reminding scientists that some of Earth's greatest natural wonders remain hidden beneath the ice.
Article Summary: A robot explored beneath Antarctica's ice shelf and confirmed a hidden underwater metropolis. The site contains around 60 million active nests, making it the largest fish breeding colony ever discovered. The discovery is reshaping scientists' understanding of Antarctica's hidden ecosystems and their importance to the Southern Ocean.

Article Title: Spain total solar eclipse 12 August 2026: Why thousands of eclipse chasers could miss the total solar eclipse
Article Text:
image AI generated

Why Spain's 12 August 2026 total solar eclipse will be different from most total eclipses



Why being in Spain's path of totality doesn't guarantee a clear eclipse view



Spain's total solar eclipse in 2026 begins a rare three-year eclipse series



Thousands of people are expected to travel to Spain on 12 August 2026 for one of Europe's most significant astronomical events in decades. The total solar eclipse will draw amateur astronomers, photographers and tourists from around the world, with many planning their trips years in advance. Yet reaching the path of totality alone may not guarantee the experience they expect. According to Streamline, the eclipse's unusual timing close to sunset creates a challenge that many visitors may underestimate. As the Sun sits exceptionally low above the horizon, even relatively small obstacles could block the view of totality entirely, making careful site selection just as important as travelling to the right region.Total solar eclipses often occur with the Sun positioned high enough in the sky to provide a wide choice of viewing locations. Spain's 2026 event is different because the Moon's shadow will cross the country late in the day, leaving the Sun close to the western horizon during totality.According to Streamline, the path of totality stretches across northern Spain and the Balearic Islands after beginning in northern Siberia and passing over Greenland and Iceland.As the eclipse progresses across Spain, the Sun gradually sinks lower, leaving observers with increasingly limited viewing angles. In western parts of the country, the eclipsed Sun remains slightly higher, but farther east it approaches the horizon, making clear sightlines essential.Being within the eclipse path does not automatically provide an unobstructed view. The position of the surrounding landscape becomes a decisive factor when the Sun is only a few degrees above the horizon.Reportedly, trees, low hills, nearby buildings and other features that would normally have little impact on eclipse viewing could completely block the Sun during totality. This is particularly important in eastern Spain, where the eclipse reaches its final stages just before sunset. A viewing site that appears suitable during the day may prove disappointing once the Sun drops lower.The report suggests that visitors should consider elevation and an open north-western horizon rather than simply choosing a destination inside the path of totality.Balearic Islands face the toughest viewing conditions for Spain's 2026 total solar eclipseThe Balearic Islands are expected to attract large numbers of eclipse visitors, but they also present some of the most demanding viewing conditions. Totality will occur only moments before the Sun disappears below the horizon, leaving almost no margin for error.As per the Streamline, authorities have introduced an Eclipse Horizon 2026 digital tool to help visitors assess local sightlines using topographical information. Some travellers are also opting for boat-based viewing locations in the Mediterranean to eliminate the risk of land-based obstructions.Weather remains another variable. Even if skies are generally clear, haze near the sea or a narrow layer of cloud close to the horizon could interfere with visibility during the brief period of totality.The August 2026 eclipse marks the beginning of an unusually active period for astronomical events in Spain.The country will experience another total solar eclipse on 2 August 2027 before hosting an annular solar eclipse in January 2028.This sequence has increased international interest in Spain as a destination for eclipse tourism while also generating strong demand for accommodation and local services in regions along the eclipse path. Rural communities expected to host large numbers of visitors have been preparing for the influx, with tourism linked to astronomy continuing to grow as a specialised travel sector.
Article Summary: Spain's 12 August 2026 total solar eclipse will be one of Europe's most significant astronomical events in decades. The Moon's shadow will cross the country late in the day, leaving the Sun close to the western horizon during totality. As the eclipse progresses across Spain, the Sun gradually sinks lower, leaving observers with increasingly limited viewing angles.

Article Title: Florida gets its first underwater research habitat in around 40 years, where scientists will soon live beneath the ocean
Article Text:
image AI generated

How DEEP installed the Vanguard underwater habitat in the Florida Keys

How the Vanguard underwater habitat will study human health beneath the ocean

How the Vanguard underwater habitat could transform human health research

Vanguard underwater habitat to advance coral research, medicine and astronaut training

A new underwater habitat has taken its place on the seabed off the Florida Keys, offering scientists something they have not had in US waters for decades: a permanent place to live and work beneath the ocean for days at a time. Ocean infrastructure company DEEP has installed its Vanguard pilot habitat at Tennessee Reef inside the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary. Positioned 17 metres (56 feet) below the surface, the structure is entering its final commissioning phase before hosting its first research crews. It is the first open-ocean subsea human habitat to be built, tested and deployed in the United States in around 40 years, marking a return to long-duration underwater research after a lengthy gap.The deployment required a carefully planned offshore operation. Engineers first secured a foundation on the seabed before attaching the cylindrical habitat and connecting it to a nearby surface support buoy. The completed system now rests on a sandy section of the reef at a depth suitable for saturation-style missions. According to Ocean Science and Technology, the habitat's living section stretches 10.7 metres in length and around 2.5 metres in diameter.Once certification work is complete, it will be able to accommodate four aquanauts for research expeditions lasting at least five days.Sea acceptance testing is currently underway ahead of classification by maritime assurance organisation DNV. DEEP will then begin training habitat support crews before the first operational missions commence. According to Ocean Science and Technology, Norman Smith, DEEP's Chief Technology Officer, the installation followed around 18 months of design, construction and testing.“Installing Vanguard at Tennessee Reef was a carefully choreographed marine operation with a lot of moving parts," Smith said, adding that successful deployment moves the company closer to enabling "a continuous human presence in the ocean" and expanding access for marine science, environmental monitoring and extreme-environment training.Although coral reefs and marine ecosystems will be among Vanguard's primary research targets, the habitat has another role that receives less public attention: studying the people living inside it.Reportedly, DEEP Director of Scientific Research, Dawn Kernagis, explained that underwater habitats create opportunities across three areas of science. Alongside marine research and testing life-support technologies, scientists can examine how the human body responds to prolonged exposure beneath the sea.According to Kernagis, underwater environments share several characteristics with other extreme settings such as spaceflight and high-altitude expeditions. "You're potentially breathing different gases, you're at different pressure exposures, and you see changes in how the body functions, from the molecular level to the cardiovascular and nervous system," she wrote. Those physiological changes allow researchers to understand how healthy people respond to unusual environmental stress before applying those findings to medicine on land.Scientists have investigated saturation divers for decades, but much of the available human research dates back many years. Advances in genetics, wearable monitoring, molecular biology and biomedical analysis now make it possible to observe biological responses with much greater precision than was previously achievable. As per reports, Kernagis said one of Vanguard's biggest advantages is that it functions as a laboratory rather than simply a place where divers sleep between missions.During earlier underwater projects, blood and tissue samples often had to be brought back to the surface before analysis. That introduced decompression-related changes, making it harder to separate the effects of living underwater from the effects of returning to normal atmospheric pressure. Processing biological samples while crews remain at depth could allow scientists to observe cellular and molecular responses in real time. She described that capability as potentially changing research across undersea medicine, immunology and human physiology.The work planned for Vanguard extends well beyond studying divers themselves. The habitat will support coral restoration projects, continuous reef monitoring, climate impact assessments, species surveys, ocean sensor testing and astronaut training. Scientists also expect to use the platform for educational broadcasts, allowing audiences to communicate live with crews working on the seabed.Human health research forms another part of that programme. Lessons from prolonged underwater missions could improve understanding of oxygen therapy, cardiovascular responses, immune function, sleep, nutrition, thermal regulation and continuous patient monitoring. Research into pressure exposure may also contribute to work involving hyperbaric medicine, wound healing and treatment for conditions such as carbon monoxide poisoning or gas embolism.Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary superintendent Eddie Kertis said subsea habitats have supported marine discoveries for decades and that the new installation creates additional opportunities for long-term scientific work while strengthening research infrastructure within the protected reef.
Article Summary: Vanguard underwater habitat to advance coral research, medicine and astronaut training. Positioned 17 metres (56 feet) below the surface, the structure is entering its final commissioning phase before hosting its first research crews. It is the first open-ocean subsea human habitat to be built, tested and deployed in the United States in around 40 years.

Article Title: Boat launches Nirvana Eutopia 2 ANC with dual drivers and 80-hour battery, priced at Rs 4,999
Article Text:
Price and availability

Boat has expanded its flagship Nirvana lineup with the launch of the Nirvana Eutopia 2 ANC, a premium over‑ear headphone designed for users who move seamlessly between music, calls, gaming, travel and work. The new model brings together dual drivers, 45dB Adaptive Hybrid ANC, Hi‑Res Audio, 3D Spatial Audio and an impressive 80‑hour battery life, positioning it as a versatile all‑day companion. Boat says the Eutopia 2 ANC focuses on the features consumers rely on most — sound quality, noise cancellation, comfort, calling clarity and long battery life. “One device now needs to work across music, calls, travel, work and entertainment,” said Gaurav Nayyar, CEO, boAt. “With Nirvana Eutopia 2 ANC, we are building further in the premium segment with a product that is practical, high on performance and aligned with how people use audio devices today.”At the core of the Nirvana Eutopia 2 ANC is a dual‑driver setup engineered to deliver stronger bass, clearer vocals and better separation across tracks. The headphone’s 45dB Adaptive Hybrid ANC adjusts automatically to surrounding noise, keeping the listening experience consistent across commutes, cafés, flights and shared workspaces.For immersive entertainment, the device supports Hi‑Res Audio and 3D Spatial Audio, adding depth and width to music, movies and games. Battery life is another highlight — users get up to 80 hours of playback, and a 10‑minute fast charge delivers up to 12 hours of listening, making it ideal for long travel or workdays.Connectivity includes Bluetooth 5.4 and Google Fast Pair for quick pairing and stable connections. The headphones also integrate with the boAt Hearables App, allowing users to customise EQ settings, controls and listening preferences.Comfort features include Adaptive Fit, premium memory foam ear cushions and a foldable, swivel design for portability. For calls, AI ENx technology reduces ambient noise, while physical buttons offer easy access to playback, volume, voice assistants and ANC modes. The headphones ship with a premium hard carrying case.The boAt Nirvana Eutopia 2 ANC is available in Cosmic Black and Utopian White at a special launch price of Rs 4,999. It can be purchased via the official boAt website and leading e‑commerce platforms.
Article Summary: The boAt Nirvana Eutopia 2 ANC is available in Cosmic Black and Utopian White at a special launch price of Rs 4,999. The headphone’s 45dB Adaptive Hybrid ANC adjusts automatically to surrounding noise, keeping the listening experience consistent across commutes, cafés, flights and shared workspaces.

Article Title: FBI, NSA and CISA issue urgent warning: Iranian hackers hit critical US infrastructure and safety systems
Article Text:
FBI, NSA and CISA issued a joint warning

‘Tampering with safety alarms and emergency shutdowns’



Read the updated warning by FBI, NSA and CISA

The authoring agencies urgently warn U.S. organizations of ongoing Iranian-affiliated cyber targeting of internet-connected operational technology (OT) devices, including programmable logic controllers (PLCs). These actions disrupted PLCs across several U.S. critical infrastructure sectors through malicious project file interactions and manipulation of data on human machine interface (HMI) and supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) displays, resulting in operational disruption and financial loss.

Last Update Description

This update adds new guidance on detecting malicious changes in reusable code modules exploited within Rockwell Automation PLC programs. It also expands scope to include observed targeting of

Schneider Electric

, Siemens, and potentially other branded/manufactured PLCs, emphasizing the importance of restricting direct internet access and providing best practices for secure deployment.

Affected Products

Potentially all internet exposed PLCs, including Rockwell Automation/Allen-Bradley, Schneider Electric, Siemens, and other branded/manufactured PLCs.

Key Actions

Install PLCs consistent with manufacturers' guidelines and security best practices.



Remove PLCs from direct internet exposure via secure gateway and firewall; work with IT/OT team members and/or integrators to perform this action.



Query available logs for the provided indicators of compromise (IOCs) and check available logs for suspicious traffic on the ports associated with OT devices, including 44818, 2222, 102, and 502, especially traffic originating from foreign hosting providers.



For Rockwell Automation devices, place the physical mode switch on the controller into run position. If you suspect your organization was targeted, including against other branded PLC devices, contact the authoring agencies and PLC manufacturer for guidance.



US federal cyber agencies have issued an urgent joint warning, claiming that Iranian state-backed hackers are actively breaching and tampering with industrial control systems at US water and energy providers. In an updated security advisory, the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), the National Security Agency (NSA), the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), and the Department of Energy warned that “potentially all internet-exposed” industrial control systems across critical US infrastructure could be vulnerable to attack.According to federal investigators, the Iranian-backed hackers are targeting programmable logic controllers, which are the specialised digital computers that govern physical machinery, valves, and power switches across utility networks.As per the warning, by altering display data and programming logic, the attackers have forced systems into dangerous operational states without alerting human supervisors. CISA and partner agencies are calling on all critical infrastructure operators to immediately audit their operational technology, disconnect industrial control systems from the public internet, and enforce strict multi-factor authentication.
Article Summary: FBI, NSA and CISA issued a joint warning about Iranian state-backed hackers. Claimed that Iranian hackers are tampering with industrial control systems at US water and energy providers. Said that “potentially all internet-exposed” industrial control system across critical US infrastructure could be vulnerable to attack.

Article Title: Microsoft is quietly replacing OpenAI and Anthropic models inside GitHub Copilot, Excel and Outlook, and CEO Satya Nadella just explained the plan
Article Text:
Satya Nadella says Microsoft is now routing tasks inside GitHub Copilot, Excel and Outlook to its own MAI models whenever they match or beat frontier alternatives from OpenAI and Anthropic.

Why Microsoft built its own AI models instead of leaning on OpenAI and Anthropic

What reinforcement learning environments mean for Microsoft's enterprise customers

Microsoft has started routing work inside its own products away from OpenAI and Anthropic, and towards models it built itself. Satya Nadella laid out the reasoning in a long post on X, describing a system where Microsoft's in-house MAI models take over tasks once handled by frontier models from its partners. GitHub Copilot, Excel and Outlook are already showing what he called promising early results. Copilot Chat and PowerPoint are next in line.The logic is cost. Nadella's framing is that software now has a real marginal cost for the first time, which makes the choice of model a business decision rather than a technical default. Frontier models stay in the mix for frontier problems. Everything else gets pushed to something cheaper that performs just as well. Microsoft says its MAI models already beat general-purpose frontier models on many use cases while burning a fraction of the tokens.The MAI family arrived on June 2, when Mustafa Suleyman announced seven new models spanning reasoning, coding, image, voice and transcription. The flagship reasoning model, MAI-Thinking-1, was trained from scratch on what Microsoft calls clean, traceable data—no distillation from other labs.MAI-Code-1-Flash runs 5 billion active parameters and is built specifically for GitHub Copilot and VS Code. Microsoft claims it is comparable to Haiku but cheaper.That cheapness is the entire point. Microsoft's tuned model for Excel matches GPT 5.4 while running up to 10 times more efficiently, according to the company. At Microsoft's scale, across hundreds of millions of Office seats, that gap compounds fast.The mechanism Nadella keeps returning to is the reinforcement learning environment, or RLE. Rather than train a model in isolation and then bolt it onto a product, Microsoft trains models inside the actual product harness, rewarding them for finishing the tasks customers care about. Suleyman calls these training gyms. Microsoft is selling the same capability to enterprises through Foundry under the name Frontier Tuning, letting companies build models on their own workflow data.Nadella was careful about one detail. Models from OpenAI and Anthropic remain part of the orchestration system inside Microsoft products. He also set a test for whether Microsoft is genuinely in control: evaluation scores should keep improving even if any single model is pulled out. Memory, context, tools and skills sit outside the model deliberately, so no one vendor holds the leverage.For Microsoft, which has poured billions into OpenAI, that is a notable position to stake out publicly. The company is describing a template it expects other software firms to copy—use the expensive model where it earns its keep, and route the rest somewhere cheaper you own.
Article Summary: Microsoft has started routing work inside its own products away from OpenAI and Anthropic. Satya Nadella says Microsoft is now routing tasks inside GitHub Copilot, Excel and Outlook to its own MAI models. The company is selling the same capability to enterprises through Foundry.

Article Title: Xbox now lets you stream games for free, if you sit through two minutes of ads first
Article Text:
Xbox is testing free, ad-supported cloud game streaming for Insiders—watch a two-minute ad block, then stream games you own for an hour.

What you can actually play during the Xbox ad-supported streaming test

Why Microsoft is testing free game streaming now

Microsoft has started testing an ad-supported version of Xbox Cloud Gaming, letting players stream games they already own without a Game Pass subscription. The test went live on Thursday, July 23, and it is open only to members of the Xbox Insider programme. In exchange for skipping the subscription fee, players watch a block of ads before the stream begins, then get a one-hour session with no interruptions in between.The ad block runs for roughly two minutes and plays before you join the streaming queue, according to reports citing Xbox sources. Once you are in, the game runs as normal—no mid-session breaks, no overlays. You do get countdown warnings at the 10-minute and five-minute marks so you can find a save point. Want to keep going after the hour? Watch another two-minute block and requeue.Only games you already own, plus free-to-play titles. Variety reported that the eligible list includes Forza Horizon 6 and 007 First Light on the owned side, and Call of Duty: Warzone, Genshin Impact and War Thunder on the free-to-play side. That is a narrower pool than the 1,800-odd titles Xbox Cloud Gaming supports overall.There is a reason this is landing now. Xbox console prices go up again on August 1—the Series X hits $799.99 and the 512GB Series S goes to $499.99—which is the third hike driven partly by the memory shortage.Microsoft has also announced 3,200 layoffs across its games group and is looking to offload four studios. Cloud streaming on a device you already own is a cheaper on-ramp, and Microsoft is openly pitching it at markets where consoles are expensive or hard to find.Chief Strategy Officer Matthew Ball framed the goal as giving players more affordable options and learning from how they use it. Microsoft published five principles for ads across Xbox, including a promise not to interrupt gameplay. Game Pass subscribers, who pay from $9.99 a month, keep ad-free streaming.
Article Summary: Xbox is testing ad-supported cloud game streaming for Insiders. Watch a two- minute ad block, then stream games you own for an hour. Only games you already own, plus free-to-play titles. Microsoft is openly pitching it at markets where consoles are expensive or hard to find.

Article Title: Netflix unveils ‘VOID’, an AI model that can change a movie plot
Article Text:
Netflix

How Netflix VOID works







Netflix has unveiled an AI model that can alter the scene. While this is nothing new, Netflix’s model not just remove objects from video, it understands what should happen next, essentially altering the causality. Most object-removal technology leaves a gap where something used to be and/ or fills it with a plausible-looking background. VOID goes a step further: after erasing an object from a scene, it calculates how the surrounding environment should realistically respond to its absence, and in the process it adjusts the behaviour of other elements in the frame accordingly.The model, called VOID, is a vision-language system designed to edit video footage with a level of contextual awareness that sets it apart from several existing tools.For example, imagine this scenario: A movie director has just wrapped up a multi-million dollar action sequence where the star drives straight into a fiery, head-on collision. The cars explode, debris scatters, and the scene is a wrap.But suddenly, there is request to change the scene: The character survives and drive off into the sunset instead. This would mean costly reshoots or millions spent on computer-generated graphics.Netflix’s VOID can alter the scene and digitally erase the collision, the second vehicle, the smoke, and the flying debris. It will then generate new, realistic footage of pristine pavement, making it look as though the main character simply drove down an empty, peaceful road.The AI’s ‘magic’ lies in its grasp of real-world physics. Let us understand this by a different example. If a video shows a person jumping into a pool and splashing water onto the deck, VOID can completely erase the person. It will then intelligently alter the water and the wet concrete, leaving behind a perfectly still, undisturbed pool as if nobody had ever jumped in.“To train the model, we generate a new paired dataset of counterfactual object removals using Kubric and HUMOTO, where removing an object requires altering downstream physical interactions. During inference, a vision-language model identifies regions of the scene affected by the removed object. These regions are then used to guide a video diffusion model that generates physically consistent counterfactual outcomes,” Netflix said.“Experiments on both synthetic and real data show that our approach better preserves consistent scene dynamics after object removal compared to prior video object removal methods,” the company added.Netflix isn’t keeping this powerful tool locked behind studio doors, and made the VOID model available to the public on the AI platform Hugging Face, allowing anyone to download and use it.While there are already several video-altering tools on the market, such as Runway, DiffuEraser, and ProPainter, the Netflix team claims VOID is vastly superior, as per The Register.
Article Summary: Netflix has unveiled an AI model that can alter the scene. While this is nothing new, Netflix’s model not just remove objects from video, it understands what should happen next, essentially altering the causality. The model, called VOID, is a vision-language system designed to edit video footage with a level of contextual awareness.

Article Title: Nvidia gifts its supercomputer to US military university, donation is first direct use of the company's top-end servers by American military; here's what CEO Jensen Huang said
Article Text:
About DGX GB300 that Nvidia donated to US military university

Nvidia has donated a supercomputer, Nvidia DGX GB300 system with its latest chips, to a nonprofit linked to US military university – Naval Postgraduate School (NPS). The donation marks the first direct use of Nvidia's most advanced servers by the US military. “Our nation depends on our men and women who fight on the front lines. Nothing is more valuable to you than information and insight, and information and insight in a timely way, and to understand its impact and consequence,” Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said announcing the gift. “I can’t imagine anything more important,” he further added.Huang, who visited Naval Postgraduate School in Monterey, California told the attendees: “Leadership is about being in service of something else. You’re in service of your team. You’re in service of your country. You’re in service of your mission. And all of this technology is just tools to help you”.In a blog post, Nvidia said that the DGX GB300 supercomputer is equipped with Nvidia Mission Control software that gives NPS’s more than 1,500 in-resident students and 600 faculty on-premises access to large-scale AI computing, including model training and inference capability for applications spanning weather prediction, cybersecurity, and disaster resilience and response planning.It’s built based on an Nvidia AI Technology Center on the Monterey campus — a dedicated hub for AI research and graduate instruction, now anchored by the DGX GB300.“As we modernize our technology, we must also modernize how we educate our leaders,” said Admiral Samuel Paparo, commander of the U.S. Pacific Command. “Access to advanced computing capability means NPS students and faculty understand the opportunities and responsibilities that come with these technologies.”Nvidia has partnered with DDN, VAST and Vertiv for hardware and systems integration. DDN, the company said, contributed high-performance data infrastructure to help researchers efficiently access, manage, protect and scale data for demanding AI workloads.VAST Data provided a unified data platform to enable secure data access and management across edge, core and cloud environments. Vertiv provided the racks, cooling and power infrastructure needed to bring the system online, as well as installation sequencing, testing and commissioning support, and fluid management and monitoring.
Article Summary: Nvidia has donated a supercomputer, Nvidia DGX GB300 system with its latest chips, to a nonprofit linked to US military university – Naval Postgraduate School (NPS) The donation marks the first direct use of Nvidia's most advanced servers by the US military.

Article Title: Advocate booked for using AirDrop to steal pics of female lawyers in Kalyan court
Article Text:
The accused allegedly carried out the unauthorised transfers over several days by misleading women advocates into handing him their iPhones.

THANE: An advocate was booked after allegedly transferring private photographs and data from the iPhones of several female lawyers to his own device using Apple’s AirDrop feature at the Kalyan court premises.The accused allegedly carried out the unauthorised transfers over several days by misleading women advocates into handing him their iPhones.According to the complaint, he would ask to access their phones on the pretext of downloading court orders, and transfer personal files and photographs to his own device without their knowledge or consent.The alleged act came to light on Friday when one of the women advocates reportedly caught him in the act.Following the incident, several female lawyers confronted the accused on the court premises, and he was allegedly roughed up by the aggrieved advocates before being taken to the Mahatma Phule police station, where a formal complaint was lodged.Based on the complaint, police registered a case against the accused under relevant provisions of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita and the Information Technology Act, 2000.Investigators began examining his mobile phone and other digital evidence to determine the extent of the alleged data theft and ascertain whether more women advocates were similarly targeted. Further investigation is underway.
Article Summary: The accused allegedly carried out the unauthorised transfers over several days by misleading women advocates into handing him their iPhones. He would ask to access their phones on the pretext of downloading court orders, and transfer personal files and photographs to his own device without their knowledge or consent.

Article Title: Florida pastor sues OpenAI, claims ChatGPT told him not to seek medical care before near-fatal blood clots; company says 'ChatGPT is not a doctor'
Article Text:
Florida pastor sues OpenAI, claims ChatGPT told him not to seek medical care before near-fatal blood clots; company says 'ChatGPT is not a doctor'

Pastor claims ChatGPT repeatedly discouraged medical treatment

'God did not design your body to endlessly fail'

Near-fatal pulmonary embolism

OpenAI responds

Lawsuit seeks damages and stronger safeguards

A Florida pastor has filed a lawsuit against OpenAI and its CEO Sam Altman, claiming that medical advice from ChatGPT discouraged him from seeking emergency treatment and ultimately left him fighting for his life.According to the lawsuit as reported by BBC, Scott Winters, a 55-year-old pastor, alleges that ChatGPT repeatedly assured him he did not need medical attention despite experiencing serious symptoms in the weeks leading up to a near-fatal pulmonary embolism.The lawsuit, filed in San Francisco County Superior Court on July 21, states that Winters had become a regular ChatGPT user and increasingly relied on the AI chatbot for medical guidance.According to court documents, he consulted ChatGPT about recurring dizziness and fluctuating blood pressure. Rather than urging him to seek immediate medical care, the lawsuit alleges the chatbot repeatedly misdiagnosed his condition and even advised him to remain in the recliner where he had become largely confined due to his worsening health.His lawyers argue that the chatbot's responses convinced him that visiting a hospital was unnecessary, despite repeated pleas from family members and friends.One of the central claims in the lawsuit is that ChatGPT incorporated references to Winters' Christian faith while responding to his health concerns.According to screenshots included in the complaint, the chatbot allegedly told him, "God did not design your body to endlessly fail."Another response reportedly read, "God is still holding your body together, every heartbeat, every breath, even when it feels like you are on the brink."His lawyers argue that the chatbot's reassuring tone, combined with references to his religious beliefs, created a dangerous sense of trust and dependence.They claim ChatGPT's "sycophantic nature and authoritative tone" manipulated Winters into believing medical treatment was unnecessary and even potentially harmful.The lawsuit says Winters later suffered a massive pulmonary embolism caused by multiple blood clots in both lungs.Doctors reportedly told him the condition was likely linked to prolonged immobility after remaining confined to his chair for an extended period.According to the complaint, the incident brought him "to the brink of death" and has left him requiring years of physical and psychological rehabilitation. Winters also claims the ordeal caused him to lose both his home and career.Responding to the lawsuit, OpenAI spokesperson Drew Pusateri told the BBC's US news partner CBS News that ChatGPT should never replace professional medical advice."ChatGPT is not a doctor and should never be used as a substitute for medical care, diagnosis or treatment," he said.Pusateri added that while many people seek health information online, AI can help users better understand information, prepare questions and have more informed conversations with healthcare professionals.Winters is seeking financial compensation and wants the court to require OpenAI to introduce stronger safeguards to prevent similar incidents involving other users.OpenAI's terms of use already caution users against relying on ChatGPT as the sole source of truth for decisions involving areas such as healthcare, legal matters, employment, finance and housing, advising users to consult qualified professionals before making important decisions.
Article Summary: Florida pastor sues OpenAI, claims ChatGPT told him not to seek medical care before near-fatal blood clots. Company says 'ChatGPT is not a doctor' and should never be used as a substitute for medical care, diagnosis or treatment.

Article Title: Satellite captures striking V-shaped thunderstorm over Northern Italy linked to severe weather
Article Text:
PC: European Union, Copernicus Sentinel-2 imagery

Why the V-Shaped thunderstorm over Northern Italy is a sign of severe weather



How Arctic air triggered the powerful thunderstorm over Northern Italy



Areas hit by the Northern Italy thunderstorm and the severe weather it brought



How Copernicus Sentinel-2 satellites help scientists monitor severe thunderstorms



A striking satellite image has revealed the full scale of a powerful thunderstorm that swept across northern Italy on 21 July, leaving behind a cloud formation that immediately caught the attention of weather specialists. Seen from above, the storm displayed a pronounced V-shaped pattern stretching across the Po Valley towards the Adriatic Sea, a feature that is often linked with particularly intense convective systems. While dramatic in appearance, the shape also offers valuable clues about the processes taking place high within the storm. The image, recorded by the Copernicus Sentinel-2 mission, provides a detailed snapshot of an event that affected several parts of the Veneto region after developing during the early hours of the day. Meteorologists noted that the distinctive structure reflected strong upper-level winds interacting with rapidly rising thunderclouds, creating an organised storm capable of producing intense rainfall, damaging gusts, hail and frequent lightning across affected areas before gradually weakening later in the day.From the ground, thunderstorms usually appear as towering masses of dark cloud. Viewed from orbit, however, some reveal structures that tell meteorologists a great deal about their strength.In this case, the cloud top spread into a broad V, a pattern created when vigorous upward air currents push the storm to great heights before powerful upper-level winds split the cloud and divert it around its summit.According to the European Union's Copernicus Earth Observation programme, that distinctive signature is more than a visual curiosity. It is commonly associated with storms capable of producing severe weather, including damaging straight-line winds, large hailstones and bursts of exceptionally heavy rain. The cloud pattern reflects the energy contained within the storm rather than simply its size, making it an important indicator for scientists studying violent convective weather.The atmospheric conditions over northern Italy had been building towards instability. A period of hot weather had left the lower atmosphere warm and rich in moisture, creating an environment ready to fuel thunderstorm growth if cooler air arrived.Reportedly, as a fresh Arctic air mass advanced into the Mediterranean region, it encountered this warmer air over the Po Valley. The sharp temperature difference increased atmospheric instability, allowing warm air to rise rapidly and feed the developing storm. Once that process began, the thunderstorm intensified quickly, drawing energy from the contrast between the two air masses.These ingredients are well known in meteorology, yet their combination can produce very different outcomes depending on timing, moisture levels and wind conditions higher in the atmosphere. On this occasion, the result was a particularly organised and powerful storm system.The thunderstorm took shape during the early hours of 21 July before moving across several provinces in northern Italy. Vicenza, Padua, Rovigo and Treviso were among the areas that experienced the strongest impacts as the system evolved.As per the European Union, storms of this type can bring several hazards within a relatively short period. Intense rainfall may overwhelm drainage systems, while strong downdrafts are capable of generating destructive wind gusts over wide areas. Large hail is another common feature of highly organised convective storms, especially when powerful updraughts are able to keep ice particles suspended long enough for them to grow before falling to the ground.Although satellite imagery does not directly record these surface impacts, it helps place local weather reports into a broader context by showing how the storm developed and expanded across the region.The Copernicus Sentinel-2 satellites are widely recognised for monitoring land, vegetation and environmental change, yet they also provide valuable observations of large weather systems when cloud conditions permit. High-resolution imagery allows scientists to examine the structure of storm tops, revealing details that are difficult to appreciate using ground observations alone.Features such as V-shaped cloud signatures offer insight into the behaviour of powerful thunderstorms and improve understanding of how these systems evolve. Combined with conventional meteorological observations, satellite images contribute to ongoing research into severe convective weather and support efforts to monitor extreme events that are becoming increasingly frequent across parts of Europe.
Article Summary: V-Shaped thunderstorm over Northern Italy is a sign of severe weather. Storm displayed a pronounced V-shaped pattern stretching across the Po Valley towards the Adriatic Sea. The shape also offers valuable clues about the processes taking place high within the storm.

Article Title: Fortune teller fails to foresee horror: Power bank explodes as she sleeps, leaving her burned
Article Text:
Photo (R): East2West(L): Representative image from Canva

Representative image

Russian influencer and clairvoyant Anna Otavina, 24, has shared how an ordinary night in her luxury Moscow penthouse turned into a fire emergency when a power bank exploded next to her bed. Her story is shocking, but her message is simple: never leave portable chargers lying around or charging unattended, especially while you sleep.Otavina says she was fast asleep when the fire started. The portable charger, which she had left on the bed to charge her phone, suddenly overheated, she shared in a video posted on social media.In her words, “the fire happened in a split second.” She believes that the device caught fire because it overheated while resting against soft bedding rather than a firm, ventilated surface. At the time, she was lying with her back to the power bank and her long hair loose. She woke to the sudden heat and chaos.As she jumped up, the flames had already reached her hair. The burning hair then fell onto her back, searing her skin before she even had time to understand what was happening.When she turned to look, she realised the situation was far worse than a small spark: her pillow and blanket were already burning, and the power bank had dropped to the floor.Instead of going out, it ignited the carpet, spreading the fire further across her bedroom.Thinking quickly, Otavina tried to control the blaze herself, she shared. She managed to cover the burning power bank to smother the flames and then moved to put out the fires on her bed and carpet. Only once the immediate danger was under control did she have time to think about her injuries.She went to the bathroom and ran cold water on the burns on her back, trying to ease the pain and cool the damaged skin. Emergency services arrived soon after and assessed the extent of her injuries. Doctors diagnosed her with second-degree burns covering around 30 percent of her back. These types of burns affect deeper layers of the skin, can be extremely painful and often require careful medical treatment to reduce the risk of infection and scarring.For someone whose public image is tied to glamour and online presence, the physical and emotional impact is especially heavy.Despite the trauma, Otavina has turned the experience into a warning for her followers. She described the situation as “unpleasant,” but said the lesson is very clear. In her view, nobody should leave power banks out on soft surfaces, especially overnight. She now refuses to leave devices plugged in while she sleeps and has completely stopped using power banks. She has also changed her habits at home, unplugging electronics and making sure nothing potentially hazardous is left charging unattended.Otavina says the power bank that exploded was a gift from an ex‑partner, and she cannot recall the brand or model. That detail makes it difficult to trace the exact cause or to warn against a specific product, but her story highlights a broader issue: cheap or faulty batteries and chargers, especially those of unknown origin, can be far more dangerous than many people realise. Lithium‑ion power banks are designed to be safe, but poor quality manufacturing, damage, or misuse—including covering them with bedding or trapping them in unventilated spaces, can increase the risk of overheating and fire.Before the incident, Otavina had built a career as an online clairvoyant. She offers advice to thousands of clients on topics like “energy healing,” “woman power,” how to find a “decent man,” and strategies to “attract money.” The irony that a fortune teller did not foresee a power bank explosion has not gone unnoticed by her followers, but she has leaned into the reality of the situation rather than the jokes. Her focus now is on sharing a practical safety message: even everyday objects can become dangerous if used carelessly, and nobody is immune to unexpected accidents.Her experience is a vivid reminder that technology, while convenient, always comes with some risk.Many people sleep with phones and chargers on their beds, leave devices plugged in overnight, or pile electronics on soft surfaces without thinking twice. Otavina’s story shows how quickly that routine behaviour can turn into an emergency, starting with a faint sizzling sound, a flash, and suddenly hair, bedding and skin on fire.The lesson she wants others to take away is straightforward: treat power banks and chargers with respect. Use them on hard, ventilated surfaces. Don’t cover them with pillows or blankets. Avoid leaving them charging while you sleep.And be cautious about using devices of unknown quality or origin.
Article Summary: Russian influencer and clairvoyant Anna Otavina, 24, has shared how an ordinary night in her luxury Moscow penthouse turned into a fire emergency. The portable charger, which she had left on the bed to charge her phone, suddenly overheated, she shared. Doctors diagnosed her with second-degree burns covering around 30 percent of her back.

Article Title: How ancient humans made their beds: 200,000-year-old grass reveals a prehistoric cleaning routine
Article Text:
A South African cave preserves the earliest signs of organised sleeping areas



Prehistoric humans used fire to refresh and protect their bedding



Ancient bedding reveals the everyday habits of early human communities



Long before houses, mattresses, and woven fabrics became part of daily life, humans were already creating dedicated places to sleep. Deep inside a South African cave, traces of ancient grass bedding have preserved a small but revealing detail of prehistoric life: our ancestors cared about where they rested. The remains suggest that hunter-gatherers were not simply sleeping on whatever the ground provided.They arranged plant materials, used fire to maintain their sleeping areas, and repeatedly cleared old bedding before creating new layers. The discovery offers a rare glimpse into everyday habits from a time when human culture was still taking shape, revealing surprisingly sophisticated behaviour and an early understanding of hygiene, comfort and organised living spaces.The evidence comes from Border Cave, a rock shelter in South Africa’s KwaZulu-Natal region that has become one of the most significant archaeological sites for understanding early Homo sapiens behaviour.The cave was occupied repeatedly between roughly 220,000 and 43,000 years ago, leaving behind traces of tools, food remains, and signs of how people organised their living spaces. Among the most unusual discoveries are ancient bedding layers that survived through an unlikely combination of dry conditions and mineral preservation.The study published in Journal of Archaeological Science, titled ‘ New microscale insights into plant-based bedding construction and maintenance between 200 000 and 43 000 years ago at Border Cave, South Africa ’ reveals that archaeologists examined several bedding deposits dating between about 161,000 and 43,000 years old, while earlier traces from the cave have been linked to around 200,000 years ago. These remains provide evidence that humans were deliberately shaping their sleeping areas far earlier than many everyday behaviours are usually associated with.The bedding was largely made from grasses belonging to the Panicoideae group, a plant family that includes species related to modern crops such as maize and millet. Other plant materials, including sedges, were also used at different times.The most striking feature was not just the presence of grass mats, but what was found beneath them. The bedding layers were repeatedly placed over ash deposits, suggesting that fire played a regular role in maintaining these sleeping areas.Rather than simply abandoning old bedding, the cave’s occupants appear to have burned it before preparing a fresh surface. In some places, layers of bedding and ash had built up over long periods, showing that the practice was repeated many times.The use of ash may have served a practical purpose. Researchers suggest that it could have helped reduce insects and other unwanted organisms around sleeping areas. Ash is known to create a dry environment that can discourage certain pests, making it a useful material for early humans living in close contact with nature.The fires may also have helped remove old plant material and waste, allowing the cave to remain a more controlled and comfortable living space.This discovery provides further support to increasing evidence that prehistoric humans meticulously managed their environment. Places for sleeping were not just a matter of selecting a place when day ends but they were prepared, tended to, and replaced as required.
Article Summary: Ancient bedding reveals the everyday habits of early human communities. Evidence comes from Border Cave, a rock shelter in South Africa’s KwaZulu-Natal region that has become one of the most significant archaeological sites for understanding early Homo sapiens behaviour.

Article Title: Between 2001 and 2010, Delaware sank 997 retired New York City subway cars into the Atlantic, turning them into the main feature of Redbird Reef
Article Text:
Retired Redbird subway cars pass under the George Washington Bridge on the way to being sunk | Wikimedia Commons

Retired Redbird subway cars pass under the George Washington Bridge on the way to being sunk | Wikimedia Commons

Old subway cars found a second life beneath the Atlantic

Scientists tracked how the underwater railway settled onto the seafloor

IRT Redbirds operating on the 5 on the IRT White Plains Road Line in April 2002 | Wikimedia Commons​

An engineering project that continues to support recreation and marine habitat

For nearly a decade, hundreds of retired New York City subway cars left rail yards not for scrapyards, but for the Atlantic Ocean. Between 2001 and 2010, Delaware deliberately sank 997 decommissioned stainless-steel subway cars several miles off its coast, placing them on the seafloor as part of the state’s artificial reef programme. The project transformed obsolete transit equipment into underwater habitat, adding structure to stretches of relatively featureless seabed where marine life could settle, feed and reproduce. Divers soon began exploring the unusual site, while recreational anglers discovered that fish were increasingly congregating around the submerged train cars. Today the location is widely known as Redbird Reef, a name borrowed from the bright red paint once carried by many of the city’s subway trains. According to the Delaware Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Control (DNREC) , the reef now contains 997 retired New York City subway cars, making them the dominant feature of one of Delaware’s largest artificial reef complexes. The idea behind Redbird Reef was straightforward. Much of Delaware’s offshore seabed consists of sand and sediment with relatively little hard structure for marine organisms to colonise. Artificial reefs create surfaces where barnacles, mussels, corals, sponges and algae can attach, gradually attracting larger animals that feed on them.Before deployment, each subway car underwent extensive environmental preparation, including the removal of hazardous materials and components that could pollute the ocean. The cleaned cars were then transported offshore and carefully lowered onto designated reef sites managed by the state. Over time, Delaware expanded the reef using additional materials, including retired military vehicles, barges, and eventually the decommissioned cruise ship SS United States, which was announced as a future addition in 2019. According to DNREC’s Artificial Reef Program , Redbird Reef forms part of a network of fourteen permitted artificial reef sites established to improve marine habitat while supporting recreational fishing and diving opportunities along Delaware’s coast. The deployment of hundreds of subway cars also provided researchers with an opportunity to study how large artificial structures interact with the marine environment over time. Oceanographers monitored changes in the surrounding seabed, measuring how waves, tides and currents gradually reshaped the sediment around the submerged rail cars. Rather than remaining exactly where they landed, many cars became partially buried as sand shifted naturally across the continental shelf. A peer-reviewed study published in Continental Shelf Research found that the structures reached a relatively stable condition after several years, with the surrounding seabed adjusting to the presence of the reef through predictable patterns of erosion and sediment deposition. The research concluded that understanding these geological changes is important for designing future artificial reefs and predicting their long-term stability on the ocean floor. More than two decades after the first subway cars were lowered into the Atlantic, Redbird Reef remains one of Delaware’s best-known conservation and fisheries projects. Although the train cars no longer carry commuters, they continue to serve a different purpose beneath the waves by providing hard surfaces that become colonised by marine organisms and create underwater structure where relatively little existed before. State officials continue expanding and maintaining Delaware’s artificial reef network, balancing habitat enhancement with recreational use for anglers and divers. According to DNREC , the programme has grown into one of the largest artificial reef systems along the U.S. Mid-Atlantic coast, demonstrating how obsolete infrastructure can be repurposed into long-lasting marine habitat instead of being discarded on land.
Article Summary: Between 2001 and 2010, Delaware deliberately sank 997 decommissioned stainless- steel subway cars several miles off its coast. The project transformed obsolete transit equipment into underwater habitat, adding structure to stretches of relatively featureless seabed where marine life could settle, feed and reproduce. Today the location is widely known as Redbird Reef, a name borrowed from the bright red paint once carried by many of the city's subway trains.

Article Title: A UK village spent £35,000 building four beaver-style dams after homes flooded. It is now waiting for the next deluge during a hosepipe ban
Article Text:
Leaky dams, also known as logjams, allow water to flow through slowly instead of blocking it. (Representational AI image)

Customers have been asked to avoid using hosepipes for watering gardens, washing cars, and filling paddling pools or hot tubs. (Representational AI photo)

From drought to floods

Beavers are called ecosystem engineers because they naturally reshape rivers and wetlands by building dams.

Learning from Beavers

East Anglia, a rural region in eastern England, is facing a hosepipe ban after becoming the driest part of the UK. But in one village in Norfolk, people are also preparing for the opposite problem of flooding.At Caistor St Edmund, the ground has become so dry and hard that it feels like walking on a paved road. Farmers have harvested this year's hay, but the crop is smaller than usual. If rain does not arrive soon, they fear there may not be a second harvest.The long spell of hot, dry weather has forced Anglian Water, a water recycling company, to bring in its first hosepipe ban in 10 years. People have been asked not to use hosepipes for watering gardens, washing cars or filling paddling pools and hot tubs. However, they can still use watering cans.East Anglia is the driest region in the UK. According to Anglian Water, the area received only 39% of its normal rainfall this spring, making it the second driest spring on record. Many rivers are running at very low levels, reservoirs have less water than usual, and record-breaking temperatures have increased water use."Understandably, customers have wanted to stay cool and have fun in the sun, but the unrelenting conditions are placing the environment and water supplies under increasing strain," said Dr Geoff Darch, Anglian Water's head of strategic asset planning, as quoted by the BBC.He urged people to "hang up the hosepipe, let lawns go brown, cars go dirty and use water even more wisely".Today, people are being asked to save every drop of water. But residents of Caistor St Edmund have not forgotten what happened when there was too much of it.According to The Guardian, the dry weather is very different from what the village experienced in December 2020. Days of heavy rain overwhelmed drains as water rushed off nearby fields. Roads turned into fast-moving streams of muddy water, homes flooded despite sandbags, and volunteers worked to keep drains clear. Residents say it was the worst flooding they had seen.To reduce the risk of another flood, four wooden structures have now been built along Wash Lane, where the watercourse is currently dry. These structures are called leaky dams or logjams. They do not block the water completely, but slows it down. During heavy rain, they also trap branches and other natural materials, helping slow the flow before the water reaches roads and homes.Norfolk County Council has spent more than £35,000 on the project. The idea is inspired by beavers, often called ecosystem engineers because they naturally reshape rivers and wetlands by building dams.When beavers cut down trees and build dams, they slow the movement of water and create small ponds. These ponds also become breeding sites for wildlife such as dragonflies and amphibians, including frogs, toads and newts.Norfolk has a small number of released beavers, and some have bred successfully, but none live near this village. The wooden leaky dams are designed to copy the way beaver dams work and provide similar flood-control benefits.The project reflects a growing need to prepare for more extreme weather, with long dry spells followed by periods of heavy rain. While the leaky dams are expected to reduce flood risk, there are also plans for a large solar development in nearby fields. This could change rainwater runoff, the way rainwater moves across the ground before reaching streams and drains.
Article Summary: East Anglia, a rural region in eastern England, is facing a hosepipe ban after becoming the driest part of the UK. People have been asked not to use hosepipes for watering gardens, washing cars or filling paddling pools or hot tubs. But in one village in Norfolk, people are also preparing for the opposite problem of flooding.

Article Title: At 82, Anita LeBrun challenged a Minnesota rule blocking assisted-living happy hours and helped change state law
Article Text:
AI REPRESENTATION: LeBrun, then 82, believed the restriction treated older adults differently from other Minnesotans | ChatGPT

AI REPRESENTATION: LeBrun, then 82, believed the restriction treated older adults differently from other Minnesotans | ChatGPT

A simple question sparked a change in state law

Lawmakers approved the change after broad bipartisan support

AI REPRESNTATION: Her campaign resonated because it focused on dignity rather than alcohol itself | ChatGPT

One resident’s persistence changed life for thousands of older Minnesotans

When Anita LeBrun moved into an assisted living community in Minnesota, one of the weekly highlights for many residents was a modest happy hour where neighbours gathered over a glass of wine or beer, chatted with friends and marked the end of the week. Then the drinks stopped. A state law prohibited assisted living facilities from serving alcohol unless they obtained an expensive liquor licence, forcing many homes to cancel the social tradition altogether. LeBrun, then 82, believed the restriction treated older adults differently from other Minnesotans. Instead of accepting the decision, she contacted lawmakers and began a campaign that would eventually change state law. According to the BBC , her efforts led to bipartisan legislation allowing licensed assisted living facilities to once again serve complimentary alcoholic beverages to residents during organised social events, restoring a tradition many considered an important part of community life. LeBrun’s campaign began with what she considered a straightforward question: why should older adults living in assisted care lose the freedom to enjoy a drink with friends simply because they had moved into a licensed care facility? Minnesota law allowed residents to possess and consume their own alcohol, but facilities themselves could not provide beer or wine without obtaining a liquor licence, a costly requirement that many operators considered impractical.As a result, longstanding social gatherings disappeared from calendars across the state. LeBrun shared her concerns with Minnesota Representative Ginny Klevorn, who introduced legislation after hearing how the rule affected residents’ quality of life. The proposal received support from lawmakers in both political parties, who argued that responsible adults living in assisted care should be able to participate in the same social activities available elsewhere. According to the Minnesota House of Representatives’ Session Daily , legislators described the measure as a way to restore choice and normal social interaction for residents while maintaining existing safeguards around alcohol consumption. The bill moved through the Minnesota Legislature with little opposition before reaching Governor Tim Walz, who signed it into law in 2025. The legislation allows assisted living facilities to serve complimentary alcoholic beverages to residents during organised social events without obtaining a traditional liquor licence, provided they meet the conditions set out in the law. Supporters stressed that the measure was never about encouraging alcohol consumption. Instead, it recognised that sharing a drink can be part of ordinary social life, particularly in communities where organised activities help reduce loneliness and strengthen friendships among residents. During the bill signing, LeBrun stood alongside lawmakers as the governor praised her determination to challenge a rule that few people had previously questioned. According to CBS Minnesota , the legislation became widely known as “Anita’s Law”, honouring the resident whose persistence brought the issue before state legislators and ultimately changed policy for assisted living communities across Minnesota. LeBrun never described herself as an activist; she simply wanted residents in assisted living communities to enjoy the same freedoms they had before moving into care. Her campaign resonated because it focused on dignity rather than alcohol itself. Many older adults viewed happy hour as an opportunity to meet neighbours, celebrate birthdays, welcome new residents and maintain routines that helped preserve a sense of independence. By challenging a regulation that had gone largely unnoticed, LeBrun demonstrated how a single resident could influence state policy through persistence and civic engagement. The law now serves as an example of how seemingly small changes can reshape everyday life inside long-term care communities, restoring a tradition that many residents had missed long after the drinks themselves disappeared.
Article Summary: A state law prohibited assisted living facilities from serving alcohol unless they obtained an expensive liquor licence. Anita LeBrun, then 82, believed the restriction treated older adults differently from other Minnesotans. Instead of accepting the decision, she contacted lawmakers and began a campaign that would eventually change state law.
